blurb: An upright, aromatic mint topped from late summer into fall with spikes of tubular raspberry-pink flowers that hummingbirds and bees mob; crush a leaf and it smells of bubblegum. Xeric, deer-proof, and built for lean, sun-baked Front Range ground.
water: Low; xeric once established — avoid wet feet
seasons: Aromatic gray-green foliage spring–fall; raspberry-pink flower spikes late summer into fall; tan seed spikes stand into winter
wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; also bumblebees and other native bees
origin: S New Mexico, W Texas & N Mexico (Chihuahua)
habitat: Rocky desert-mountain slopes, granite-cliff crevices & canyons, ~5,000–6,000 ft

care.hardiness: Reliably cold-hardy across the Front Range — USDA zones 5–9 (Boulder is ~5b–6a), enduring temperatures to about −20°F where it has the sharp drainage it demands.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost into lean, fast-draining ground; give it the hottest, best-drained spot you have and do not amend with rich compost. It grows well in containers when garden soil is too heavy — use a gritty, fast-draining mix, never let the pot stay wet, and overwinter potted crowns in a protected spot, since roots in a pot are far less cold-hardy than in open ground.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — six-plus hours — for dense, upright, well-colored flower spikes; it flops and blooms shyly in shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Wants lean, sharply drained soil with a near-neutral pH; thrives on dry, rocky, low-fertility ground and resents Boulder's heavy wet clay, where the crown rots. Do not over-amend.[1][2]
care.water: Low and xeric once established — water new transplants regularly the first season, then taper to deep, infrequent soakings. Overwatering, especially in poorly drained soil, is the main way to kill it.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 18 in apart; clumps reach roughly 2–3 ft tall and 1.5–2 ft wide.[2]
care.propagation: Grow from seed started indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost at about 55–65°F, or direct-sow after frost; it also self-sows modestly. To clone a plant, take softwood cuttings from non-flowering shoots in late spring/early summer, or divide an established clump in early spring.[1][3]
care.bloom: Spikes of tubular, sweetly bubblegum-scented raspberry-pink to lavender-pink flowers from late summer into fall (about July–September here) — peak nectar just as many summer perennials fade. Deadhead spent spikes to neaten and prolong bloom.[1][2]
care.feeding: No regular feeding needed in normal soil; if anything, a single light application in fall. Rich feeding produces floppy, short-lived growth.[2]
care.maintenance: Low. Leave the stems standing over winter — they shelter the crown and improve hardiness — then cut old growth back to about 4–5 in above the ground in mid-spring as new shoots emerge.[2]
care.troubles: Few pests or diseases; the real risk is root and crown rot in wet, poorly drained, or over-amended soil. Sharp drainage and restraint with water prevent it.[1][2]
care.companions: A hummingbird magnet for hot, dry borders and rock gardens — pair with other xeric, lean-soil plants such as penstemons, salvias, and ornamental grasses.[2]

references.name: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Agastache (Hummingbird Mint)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Agastache cana

edible.summary: Grown mainly as an ornamental hummingbird plant; the aromatic leaves (and dried flowers) are used to brew a minty herbal tea and flavor dishes.[3]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and dried flower petals are the parts used — steeped fresh or dried for a mint-and-bubblegum-scented herbal tea, or used in small amounts to add a minty note to cooking.[3]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves and dried flowers as a flavoring or tea, not as a vegetable — steep a few clean leaves in hot water. This is a culinary/herbal-tea use of a fragrant mint, not a bulk food.[3]
edible.caution: Agastache cana is grown chiefly as an ornamental, and its food use is limited to leaves and dried flowers as a mint-like tea/flavoring — there is no documented edible use for any other part, so don't eat the roots, stems, or seeds. As with any aromatic mint-family herb, use it in modest culinary amounts and be confident of the identification before brewing.[3]
